heroOverview: Chancroid is a sexually transmitted bacterial infection caused by Haemophilus ducreyi that produces one or more painful genital ulcers and, in roughly half of cases, enlargement of inguinal lymph nodes that can rupture through the skin. Unlike syphilitic chancres, which are classically painless, chancroid ulcers are characteristically very painful, with a soft base and ragged, undermined edges. The disease is now rare in high-income countries — fewer than 30 cases are reported annually in the United States — but remains endemic in sub-Saharan Africa, parts of South and Southeast Asia, and Latin America, where it affects predominantly young adults in sex-worker networks. Because H. ducreyi genital ulcers disrupt the mucosal barrier, chancroid is a recognised cofactor that amplifies HIV transmission and acquisition approximately threefold. A single dose of azithromycin or a three-day course of ciprofloxacin cures the infection in most immunocompetent patients.

keyStats.prevalence: Fewer than 30 reported cases per year in the United States since the late 1990s; endemic in parts of sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and Latin America
keyStats.demographics: Predominantly affects men who have sex with female sex workers; uncircumcised men at higher risk; women more often asymptomatic carriers
keyStats.avgAge: Peak incidence in sexually active adults aged 20–40 years
keyStats.globalCases: Global burden estimated at approximately 7 million cases annually (WHO), mainly in low- and middle-income endemic countries

definition: Chancroid (ICD-10: A57) is a genital ulcer disease caused by the fastidious Gram-negative facultative anaerobic bacillus Haemophilus ducreyi. The organism is transmitted almost exclusively through direct contact with an infected ulcer during sexual intercourse. After an incubation period of 3–10 days, the bacterium causes a papule that breaks down into a painful ulcer with a soft, necrotic, grey or yellow exudate-covered base and irregular, undermined edges — features that contrast starkly with the indurated, painless ulcer of primary syphilis. The inguinal lymph nodes draining the infection site enlarge in 30–60% of cases, sometimes forming a fluctuant mass called a bubo that can spontaneously rupture and discharge pus through the skin. Chancroid falls under infectious disease and sexual health medicine. The disease is a major public health concern in endemic settings principally because genital ulcers dramatically increase both the risk of acquiring and the risk of transmitting HIV, by disrupting epithelial barriers and recruiting activated immune cells to the ulcer surface. Co-infection with syphilis and herpes simplex virus is common and must be actively excluded at diagnosis.

typesClassification.type: Classic painful genital ulcer without bubo
typesClassification.description: The most common presentation: one or more soft, tender, necrotic ulcers on the genitals without detectable lymph node enlargement, occurring in roughly 40–50% of symptomatic cases.
Painful genital ulcer with unilateral or bilateral tender inguinal adenopathy; the bubo becomes fluctuant in about 50% of adenopathy cases and may rupture spontaneously, draining pus.
Occasionally affects peri-anal tissue, inner thighs, or oral mucosa through direct contact; may lack the classic ulcer appearance and is more often misdiagnosed.
Women may harbour H. ducreyi and transmit the organism through ulcers or intact mucosa with minimal or absent symptoms, playing an important role in maintaining endemic transmission chains.

primarySymptoms: One or more painful, soft-based genital ulcers with irregular, ragged, undermined edges — the tenderness distinguishes chancroid from the painless indurated chancre of primary syphilis.
A grey or yellowish necrotic slough or purulent exudate coating the base of the ulcer, contrasting with the clean base of a herpetic erosion.
Marked local tenderness on and around the ulcer such that touching or examination is acutely painful; dyspareunia and dysuria are common.
Painful, unilateral inguinal lymphadenopathy (bubo) in 30–60% of symptomatic patients, typically developing 1–2 weeks after the genital ulcer appears.
Fluctuance of the inguinal lymph node mass as pus accumulates, which may then rupture spontaneously through the overlying skin and drain a creamy discharge.
Oedema of the overlying inguinal skin and subcutaneous tissue around the bubo, sometimes with erythema of the skin directly over the node.
Multiple ulcers in some cases: 'kissing lesions' can form on opposing skin surfaces when an initial ulcer autoinoculates an adjacent area through direct contact.
In women, ulcers frequently occur on the labia, fourchette, or cervix; cervical ulcers may be asymptomatic or cause only a vaginal discharge.
Low-grade systemic malaise, fever, or inguinal pain that precedes or accompanies the bubo in more advanced cases.
Dysuria or urethral discharge (in men) when ulcers involve the urethral meatus or extend into the urethra.
earlyWarningSigns: A small, tender red papule or pustule on the genitals appearing 3–10 days after a potentially risky sexual exposure.
Rapid breakdown of a papule into an open, painful ulcer over 24–48 hours.
New groin tenderness or a small tender lump appearing 1–2 weeks after a genital sore.
Any painful genital sore in a person who lives in or has recently visited an endemic country or who has had sexual contact with sex workers.
emergencySigns: A rapidly enlarging, fluctuant inguinal mass threatening imminent rupture, which needs same-day assessment for aspiration or incision to prevent spontaneous fistula formation.
Signs of secondary infection or cellulitis around the ulcer or inguinal lymph node with systemic fever, tachycardia, or malaise.
Very extensive destructive ulceration of the genitalia (phagedenic chancroid) with rapidly progressing tissue necrosis, rare but requiring urgent inpatient care.
Co-existing clinical features of primary syphilis (which may be co-present) with neurological symptoms, as combined untreated syphilis carries significant morbidity.

causes.description: Chancroid is acquired almost exclusively through direct sexual contact with an infected genital ulcer. The bacterium invades through microscopic skin breaks, produces cytotoxins (CDT — cytolethal distending toxin), and evades local innate immunity to establish a necrotising inflammatory focus.

Unprotected sex with multiple partners, sex workers, or in an endemic setting dramatically raises acquisition risk. The organism's infectious dose is low, and a single contact with an active ulcer is sufficient for transmission.

Uncircumcised men are at significantly higher risk, probably because the inner preputial surface provides a warm, moist environment that supports H. ducreyi colonisation and because micro-abrasions are more common under the foreskin during intercourse.

Pre-existing genital ulcers from herpes simplex or syphilis disrupt the epithelial barrier and facilitate H. ducreyi entry, while chancroid ulcers in turn amplify HIV acquisition by approximately three-fold by concentrating activated CD4 cells at the ulcer surface.

HIV infection and other immunosuppressive states impair local and systemic control of H. ducreyi, increasing susceptibility to chancroid, slowing ulcer healing, and raising the likelihood of atypical presentations.

riskFactors.factor: Unprotected sexual intercourse with an infected partner
riskFactors.description: Condoms substantially reduce but do not completely eliminate transmission risk; consistent condom use is the most directly modifiable prevention behaviour.

riskFactors.factor: Travel to or residence in an endemic region
riskFactors.description: Sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, India, and parts of Latin America still have endemic transmission; travel-related chancroid clusters occur in high-income countries.

Multiple studies show uncircumcised men have two- to threefold higher chancroid acquisition rates than circumcised men; adult circumcision is a recognised HIV/STI prevention strategy in endemic settings.

riskFactors.factor: Commercial sex work involvement (as client or provider)
riskFactors.description: Sex-worker networks sustain H. ducreyi transmission chains; targeted prevention and screening programmes in this population have dramatically reduced incidence in some endemic cities.

HIV-positive individuals are more susceptible to chancroid, experience more severe or atypical ulcers, and may respond more slowly to treatment; HIV and chancroid co-infection amplifies onward HIV transmission.
A higher partner count increases the probability of exposure to an infected individual; consistent partner reduction and notification remain the key public health levers.

riskFactors.factor: Low socioeconomic status and limited healthcare access
riskFactors.description: Chancroid thrives where STI services, syndromic management, and partner treatment are unavailable; poverty-related barriers to treatment prolong infectiousness.
Existing herpes simplex or syphilis ulcers break epithelial integrity and increase susceptibility to H. ducreyi; concurrent STIs must be evaluated at every diagnosis.

affectedDemographics: Sexually active adults aged 20–40 in endemic regions
Clients and providers of commercial sex work
People co-infected with HIV, particularly in sub-Saharan Africa and Southeast Asia
Travellers returning from endemic countries who have had unprotected sex
Partners of individuals with confirmed chancroid diagnosis
diagnosisOverview: No single test has both high sensitivity and high specificity for chancroid; the diagnosis is often syndromic in resource-limited endemic settings. In high-income countries the CDC defines a probable case as: one or more painful genital ulcers, no evidence of T. pallidum on darkfield or serology, and no evidence of herpes simplex on testing, or negative HSV PCR. Definitive diagnosis requires isolation of H. ducreyi from the ulcer; the organism is fastidious and requires specialised culture media (enriched chocolate agar with vancomycin) with sensitivity of only 60–80%. PCR-based tests are available in some reference laboratories and have higher sensitivity, but are not widely accessible. Ulcer swabs should be taken from the undermined edge or base after gently removing the exudate. Because H. ducreyi PCR is not universally available and the clinical overlap with HSV and syphilis is substantial, most guidelines recommend simultaneous testing for both: syphilis serology plus RPR, HSV PCR or culture, and HIV serology. Dark-field microscopy of ulcer exudate can exclude syphilis when performed by an experienced microscopist. In endemic settings where laboratory confirmation is unavailable, syndromic management — treating simultaneously for chancroid, syphilis, and herpes based on clinical features — is the recommended approach and has proven effective at reducing population-level STI burden.

diagnosticTests.test: Ulcer swab for H. ducreyi culture
diagnosticTests.purpose: Definitive identification of the causative organism; the gold standard, though sensitivity is only 60–80% because H. ducreyi is a fastidious bacterium that needs specialised media and conditions.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A clinician gently cleans the ulcer base and swabs the undermined edge; the sample is placed on enriched chocolate agar plates. Results take 2–5 days.

diagnosticTests.test: H. ducreyi PCR (multiplex genital ulcer PCR)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Higher sensitivity than culture (approximately 95%) and can simultaneously detect H. ducreyi, T. pallidum, and herpes simplex; used in reference laboratories and endemic-setting research.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: An ulcer swab is sent to a molecular laboratory; results are available in 24–72 hours depending on the facility.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes syphilis, the most important differential for a painless-or-mixed genital ulcer, which requires different treatment and has serious long-term consequences if missed.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood draw from a vein; both tests should be requested simultaneously. RPR results are available in hours; confirmatory TPHA in 24 hours.

diagnosticTests.test: HSV PCR or culture from the ulcer
diagnosticTests.purpose: Herpes simplex causes painful genital ulcers that can closely resemble chancroid; HSV PCR is the most sensitive test for both primary and recurrent herpes.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A swab from the ulcer base or vesicle fluid is sent for molecular testing; results in 24–48 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies co-infection that changes treatment decisions and risk assessment; chancroid and HIV are strongly co-associated, and untreated HIV impairs chancroid healing.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood draw for HIV antibody/antigen combination test; results in 30 minutes with rapid tests or 24 hours on standard assay.

diagnosticTests.purpose: A rapid but low-sensitivity bedside test that may show the characteristic 'school of fish' parallel arrangement of Gram-negative coccobacilli in positive cases.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A swab of ulcer exudate is smeared on a glass slide and stained; takes 10–15 minutes. Sensitivity is approximately 50%, so a negative result does not exclude chancroid.

treatmentOverview: Chancroid responds well to several antibiotic regimens. The CDC and WHO recommend as first-line: a single oral dose of azithromycin 1 g, or a single intramuscular injection of ceftriaxone 250 mg, or a seven-day course of erythromycin 500 mg three times daily, or a three-day course of ciprofloxacin 500 mg twice daily. Single-dose regimens (azithromycin or ceftriaxone) are preferred for adherence and programmatic reasons. Azithromycin is particularly favoured in endemic settings because a single observed dose ensures cure without risk of incomplete treatment. HIV-positive patients may respond more slowly, and some guidelines suggest a seven-day erythromycin course or repeat azithromycin in this group when clinical response at one week is incomplete. Fluctuant buboes should not be incised or undergo standard drainage; needle aspiration through adjacent healthy skin is the preferred approach to reduce the risk of fistula formation, and may need to be repeated. Incision and drainage are reserved for cases where aspiration fails to control the bubo. All sexual contacts within 10 days before symptom onset should be traced and offered treatment regardless of symptoms. Concurrent syphilis and herpes should be treated where detected or clinically suspected. HIV testing is recommended for all patients, and HIV-positive patients should be referred for management of both conditions. Barrier contraception should be reinforced. Test-of-cure is not routinely required when single-dose azithromycin or ceftriaxone is used and the clinical response is adequate at 3–7 days.

medicalTreatments.name: Azithromycin (single dose)
medicalTreatments.description: A macrolide antibiotic given as a single oral 1 g dose; the preferred regimen in most guidelines because single-dose administration removes adherence barriers, achieves high tissue concentrations, and is safe in pregnancy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical cure in approximately 88–93% of immunocompetent patients in randomised trials; potentially lower in HIV-positive patients where a seven-day course is preferred.

medicalTreatments.name: Ceftriaxone (single IM dose)
medicalTreatments.description: A third-generation cephalosporin given as a single intramuscular injection of 250 mg; particularly useful where oral adherence is uncertain or when a patient needs treatment at a single clinic visit.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Equivalent single-dose cure rates to azithromycin at approximately 88–92% in immunocompetent individuals.

medicalTreatments.name: Ciprofloxacin (three-day course)
medicalTreatments.description: A fluoroquinolone given as 500 mg orally twice daily for three days; highly effective but contraindicated in pregnancy and in areas where fluoroquinolone resistance has been documented in H. ducreyi isolates.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cure rates of approximately 85–90% in susceptible strains; resistance has been reported in Asia, requiring local susceptibility data to guide use.

medicalTreatments.name: Erythromycin (seven-day course)
medicalTreatments.description: 500 mg orally three times daily for seven days; used as an alternative when first-line agents are contraindicated or unavailable, and as the preferred regimen in HIV-positive patients where extended therapy is recommended.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cure rates approximately 80–85%; the longer course improves outcomes in HIV-positive patients where healing is slower.

medicalTreatments.name: Needle aspiration of fluctuant bubo
medicalTreatments.description: For enlarging, fluctuant inguinal lymph node collections, aspiration through adjacent normal skin reduces the risk of spontaneous fistula formation and relieved pressure-related pain; performed under local anaesthesia.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces fistula formation and accelerates resolution; approximately 80–90% of aspirated buboes do not require further procedural intervention when the antibiotic is effective.

surgicalOptions.name: Incision and drainage of bubo
surgicalOptions.description: Reserved for fluctuant buboes that do not resolve with antibiotics and needle aspiration; performed under local anaesthesia with wide incision to ensure complete drainage.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Effective at draining pus but carries a 10–30% risk of fistula formation and wound healing delay; aspiration is preferred as first-line procedural management.

alternativeTreatments.name: Syndromic management (endemic settings)
alternativeTreatments.description: Where laboratory testing is unavailable, simultaneous empirical treatment for chancroid and syphilis (and sometimes herpes) based on the clinical appearance of a painful ulcer; WHO-endorsed and has measurably reduced STI burden in programmatic use.

alternativeTreatments.name: Partner treatment and tracing
alternativeTreatments.description: Ensuring sexual contacts are treated even if asymptomatic is as important as treating the index case; untreated partners reinfect the primary patient and sustain community transmission.

whySeeSpecialist: An infectious disease physician or sexual health specialist should be involved whenever the ulcer does not begin to heal within 7 days of appropriate antibiotics (raising concern for resistance, co-infection, or HIV immunosuppression), when the patient is HIV-positive, when the clinical picture does not clearly differentiate chancroid from syphilis, or when a fluctuant bubo requires aspiration. Sexual health clinics are best placed to simultaneously manage contact tracing and test for all relevant co-infections.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek care at a sexual health or genitourinary medicine clinic where comprehensive STI panel testing, partner notification support, and experienced clinicians are available. Ask whether the clinic can perform H. ducreyi PCR or has access to a reference laboratory. If HIV co-infection is confirmed, integrated care with HIV medicine is important. For travel-related chancroid, clinics with tropical medicine expertise are valuable.
preventionStrategies: Use condoms consistently and correctly — they significantly reduce but do not eliminate H. ducreyi transmission during genital contact.
Reduce the number of sexual partners and avoid unprotected sex with partners of unknown STI status.
Male circumcision reduces transmission risk by approximately 50% in endemic settings according to randomised trials.
Seek immediate assessment and treatment for any painful genital sore, particularly after high-risk exposure.
Notify and treat sexual contacts within the 10 days preceding symptom onset.
In endemic settings, regular STI screening for sex workers and their contacts has measurably reduced chancroid prevalence when combined with targeted treatment.
lifestyleModifications: Abstain from sexual intercourse until the ulcer is fully healed and partners have been treated.
Maintain genital hygiene by gentle washing to reduce secondary infection of open ulcers.
Avoid trauma to the ulcer area — tight clothing or friction can delay healing and promote autoinoculation.
Disclose your diagnosis to recent sexual partners so they can be tested and treated.
If HIV-positive, begin or continue antiretroviral therapy as directed — it reduces the severity of chancroid and decreases the risk of onward HIV transmission.

recommended: Adequate protein and micronutrients to support ulcer healing and immune function
Sufficient fluids to maintain hydration, particularly with fever
Vitamin C-rich foods to support collagen synthesis and tissue repair
Zinc-containing foods (nuts, seeds, meat) which support wound healing
avoid: Alcohol during antibiotic treatment, particularly with erythromycin which can cause gastrointestinal side effects
Ciprofloxacin taken simultaneously with dairy or calcium-fortified foods, which can reduce its absorption
Food or drink that could irritate already-inflamed genital or inguinal tissue through immune or inflammatory pathways

exerciseGuidelines: Rest is advisable during the acute phase when a bubo is fluctuant or pain is severe. Physical activity that causes friction on the ulcer — cycling, running, or activities requiring close-fitting clothing — should be temporarily avoided to prevent trauma and autoinoculation. Light walking is appropriate once acute pain is controlled. Full resumption of physical activity is appropriate once the ulcer has healed, typically 2–4 weeks after starting effective antibiotics.
dailyManagement: Take your full prescribed antibiotic course without interruption; single-dose regimens require no further doses, but multi-day courses must be completed.
Keep the ulcer clean with gentle washing — do not apply topical antiseptics, which can delay healing.
Avoid touching the ulcer with unwashed hands, and do not touch eyes or other mucous membranes after contact with the lesion to prevent autoinoculation.
Return for review within 3–7 days to confirm the ulcer is healing; failure to improve warrants culture for resistance and HIV testing.
Notify all sexual partners from the 10 days preceding your symptoms; your clinic can assist with discreet partner notification if needed.
prognosis: With appropriate antibiotic therapy, most chancroid ulcers in immunocompetent patients begin healing within 3–7 days and resolve completely within 2–4 weeks, leaving no permanent scarring in the majority of cases. Larger ulcers and buboes take longer to resolve. The main risks of inadequate treatment are secondary infection of the ulcer, fistula formation from a ruptured bubo, and — critically — greatly amplified HIV transmission risk during the period of open ulceration. In HIV-positive patients, healing is slower and a longer antibiotic course may be needed; response should be reassessed at 7 days. Ciprofloxacin resistance has emerged in some Asian isolates; azithromycin or ceftriaxone are preferred where resistance is suspected. Recurrence after successful treatment and reinfection-free abstinence is uncommon; recurrences usually represent reinfection from an untreated partner rather than treatment failure.
recoveryTimeline: Subjective improvement in pain typically occurs within 2–3 days of starting effective treatment. Ulcer size begins to reduce by day 3–7; small ulcers often heal completely within 2 weeks, while larger lesions may take 3–4 weeks. Inguinal lymphadenopathy and bubo may take longer to resolve and, if fluctuant, often require aspiration to accelerate resolution. Full healing without scar formation is the expected outcome for most uncomplicated cases in immunocompetent patients.
complications: Inguinal bubo formation and spontaneous rupture — if untreated, the fluctuant lymph node can rupture through the skin, creating a chronically draining fistula that is slow to close.
Phagedenic chancroid — a rare but severe form in which rapidly progressive tissue necrosis destroys large areas of the genitalia; strongly associated with HIV co-infection.

HIV acquisition and transmission amplification — genital ulcers increase HIV acquisition risk approximately threefold and transmission risk by a similar magnitude by disrupting epithelial barriers and concentrating activated CD4 T cells.
Co-infection with syphilis or herpes simplex — present in approximately 10–30% of chancroid diagnoses; both carry independent long-term risks if missed.
Phimosis or urethral scarring — repeated or large penile ulcers can cause fibrotic narrowing of the prepuce or, rarely, the urethra.
Psychological and social impact — stigma associated with STI diagnosis can cause significant anxiety, relationship disruption, and barriers to care-seeking.

supportResources.name: CDC — Chancroid Treatment Guidelines
supportResources.description: U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention clinical guidelines for chancroid diagnosis and treatment, including first-line antibiotic recommendations.

supportResources.name: WHO — Genital Ulcer Disease
supportResources.description: WHO syndromic management guidelines for STIs including genital ulcer disease caused by H. ducreyi, designed for use in resource-limited endemic settings.

supportResources.name: BASHH — UK Guidelines on the Management of Chancroid
supportResources.description: British Association for Sexual Health and HIV guidelines covering chancroid diagnosis, treatment, and partner management in a UK context.

sources.title: CDC Sexually Transmitted Infections Treatment Guidelines, 2021 — Chancroid

confusedWithConditions.name: Syphilis (primary chancre)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: The primary syphilitic chancre is classically painless with a clean, indurated base, whereas chancroid ulcers are acutely painful with a soft, necrotic, purulent base — though co-infection makes clinical distinction unreliable, requiring laboratory testing.

confusedWithConditions.name: Herpes simplex (genital herpes)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Herpes begins as vesicles that erode into multiple shallow, painful ulcers with a clean base and no lymph node fluctuance; chancroid ulcers are deeper, have undermined edges, and produce a purulent exudate. HSV PCR distinguishes them definitively.

confusedWithConditions.name: Gonorrhoea
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Gonorrhoea causes urethral or cervical discharge without a true genital ulcer, whereas chancroid produces a destructive, painful soft-based ulceration.

relatedConditions.name: Syphilis
relatedConditions.relevance: Primary syphilis produces a painless genital ulcer (chancre) that is the most critical clinical differential for chancroid; co-infection with both pathogens is well documented in endemic settings.

relatedConditions.name: Herpes simplex infection
relatedConditions.relevance: The most prevalent cause of painful genital ulcers globally; HSV and H. ducreyi co-infection occurs and requires simultaneous testing at presentation.

relatedConditions.name: Gonorrhoea
relatedConditions.relevance: Frequently co-present in high-risk sexual networks where chancroid is endemic; concomitant screening is standard practice.

relatedConditions.name: Trichomoniasis
relatedConditions.relevance: A common co-occurring STI in populations where chancroid is endemic; concurrent treatment and screening reduce overall STI burden.

faqs.question: What does a chancroid ulcer look like?
faqs.answer: A chancroid ulcer is typically a soft, painful sore with irregular, ragged, undermined edges and a grey or yellowish pus-covered base. It is usually very tender to touch, unlike the painless sore of syphilis. Multiple ulcers can form when the first infects an adjacent skin surface.

faqs.question: How is chancroid different from syphilis?
faqs.answer: The key difference is pain and texture: chancroid ulcers are intensely painful with soft, necrotic bases, while syphilitic chancres are classically painless with a firm, indurated border. Both are genital ulcers, but they require different treatments and lab tests distinguish them definitively.

faqs.question: How do you get chancroid?
faqs.answer: Chancroid is transmitted through direct sexual contact with an infected genital ulcer. The Haemophilus ducreyi bacterium enters through a small break in the skin or mucous membrane during intercourse. It is not spread through casual contact, toilet seats, or shared objects.

faqs.question: Can chancroid be cured?
faqs.answer: Yes. Chancroid is fully curable with antibiotics. A single dose of azithromycin or a single injection of ceftriaxone cures most cases in immunocompetent people. The ulcer begins to heal within 3–7 days of effective treatment and resolves completely within 2–4 weeks in most cases.

faqs.question: Is chancroid common in the United States?
faqs.answer: No — chancroid is now very rare in the U.S., with fewer than 30 cases reported per year since the late 1990s. It remains endemic in parts of sub-Saharan Africa, South and Southeast Asia, and Latin America. U.S. cases are typically travel-related or linked to specific high-risk networks.

faqs.question: What is a bubo in chancroid?
faqs.answer: A bubo is a painful, swollen inguinal lymph node that develops in 30–60% of people with chancroid, typically 1–2 weeks after the genital ulcer appears. It can become filled with pus (fluctuant) and may rupture through the skin. Treatment is antibiotics plus needle aspiration if the node is fluctuant.

faqs.question: Does chancroid cause HIV risk?
faqs.answer: Yes. Active chancroid ulcers increase the risk of acquiring or transmitting HIV approximately threefold because they disrupt the skin and mucosal barrier and attract activated immune cells to the surface. Treating chancroid promptly reduces this risk significantly. HIV testing is recommended for everyone diagnosed with chancroid.

faqs.question: How is chancroid diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Chancroid is diagnosed clinically when a painful soft-based genital ulcer is present with no evidence of syphilis or herpes on testing. Definitive diagnosis requires culture or PCR for Haemophilus ducreyi, though lab confirmation is not always available. Most guidelines recommend simultaneous testing for syphilis, herpes, and HIV.

faqs.question: What antibiotic treats chancroid?
faqs.answer: First-line options are a single 1 g oral dose of azithromycin or a single 250 mg intramuscular injection of ceftriaxone. A three-day oral course of ciprofloxacin and a seven-day course of erythromycin are also effective alternatives. HIV-positive patients may need a longer course or repeat treatment.

faqs.question: Should sexual partners be treated?
faqs.answer: Yes. All sexual contacts in the 10 days before your symptoms started should be evaluated and treated even if they have no ulcer, because women in particular can carry H. ducreyi without symptoms. Treating partners prevents reinfection and breaks the transmission chain.

faqs.question: Can chancroid come back after treatment?
faqs.answer: Chancroid does not typically recur after successful treatment in the absence of reinfection. Recurrent ulcers in a treated patient almost always represent reinfection from an untreated partner, rather than antibiotic failure. Partner treatment and safe sex practices after healing are essential.

faqs.question: Is chancroid dangerous?
faqs.answer: Chancroid is curable and rarely fatal in healthy people, but it carries serious risks if left untreated: large painful ulcers, ruptured lymph node abscesses, rare tissue destruction, and a threefold increase in HIV risk during active ulceration. Prompt treatment eliminates these risks.

faqs.question: Can chancroid affect women differently?
faqs.answer: Women more often have atypical or internal ulcers — on the cervix, vaginal wall, or labia — that may cause little pain and go unnoticed. Women can carry and transmit H. ducreyi asymptomatically, which is why partner screening is important even when a female contact has no visible sores.

faqs.question: Does male circumcision protect against chancroid?
faqs.answer: Studies in endemic settings show uncircumcised men have two to three times the chancroid acquisition risk of circumcised men. The warm moist environment under the foreskin supports H. ducreyi colonisation. Circumcision is a recognised HIV and STI prevention strategy in high-prevalence settings, though it does not replace condom use.

faqs.question: How long does it take for a chancroid ulcer to heal?
faqs.answer: With effective antibiotics, pain typically improves within 2–3 days and the ulcer begins to shrink by day 3–7. Small ulcers heal within 2 weeks; larger ulcers may take 3–4 weeks. Buboes can take longer and may need aspiration. If there is no improvement in 7 days, return for review.

faqs.question: Can I test for chancroid at home?
faqs.answer: No reliable home test for chancroid is available. Diagnosis requires laboratory culture or PCR from an ulcer swab, ideally performed at a sexual health clinic. Home STI kits typically test for HIV, chlamydia, gonorrhoea, and syphilis but not H. ducreyi. Any painful genital sore should be assessed in person by a clinician.

faqs.question: What is phagedenic chancroid?
faqs.answer: Phagedenic chancroid is a rare severe form in which the ulcer progresses rapidly, destroying large areas of genital tissue. It is strongly associated with HIV co-infection and requires inpatient care with intravenous antibiotics and close surgical monitoring. It is uncommon in immunocompetent patients receiving prompt treatment.

faqs.question: Is chancroid the same as a herpes sore?
faqs.answer: No. Both cause painful genital ulcers, but they are different infections requiring different treatment. Herpes is caused by a virus (HSV-2 or HSV-1), begins as vesicles, recurs lifelong, and has a clean shallow base. Chancroid is caused by a bacterium (H. ducreyi), does not recur after cure, and produces a deeper ulcer with a pus-covered base.

faqs.question: When should I see a doctor for a genital sore?
faqs.answer: See a doctor as soon as you notice a genital sore, regardless of whether it hurts — painful ulcers suggest chancroid or herpes, while painless ones may be syphilis, but clinical appearances overlap and only testing distinguishes them. Early treatment prevents complications, reduces HIV risk, and protects partners.

faqs.question: Does chancroid cause long-term fertility problems?
faqs.answer: Uncomplicated chancroid treated promptly does not typically cause long-term fertility problems. However, severe or repeated infection causing extensive scarring of the urethra or pelvic structures could theoretically affect fertility, as could concurrent syphilis or gonorrhoea if untreated. Prompt treatment and partner notification minimise all such risks.

metaDescription: Chancroid causes painful genital ulcers from H. ducreyi bacteria and increases HIV risk threefold. Learn symptoms, diagnosis and single-dose antibiotic cure.
